examples.explanation: Cities: 0,1,2. Start at 0. Permutations of [1,2]: (1,2): path 0→1→2→0 = dist(0,1)+dist(1,2)+dist(2,0) = 1+3+2 = 6. (2,1): path 0→2→1→0 = 2+3+1 = 6. Minimum = 6.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n!)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of cities to visit and compute the total distance for each path, keeping track of the minimum. Use this for small n (e.g., n <= 10) due to factorial growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force TSP with Permutations
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of city visits and compute minimum total distance
ladders.explanation: The Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) asks: given n cities with distances between each pair, find the shortest possible route that visits each city exactly once and returns to the starting city. The brute force approach enumerates all possible tours by generating every permutation of cities, calculating the total distance for each permutation, and tracking the minimum. This works because it exhaustively checks every possible solution, guaranteeing we find the optimal one. The algorithm starts at city 0 (or any fixed starting point), generates all permutations of the remaining n-1 cities, computes the total distance for each permutation (including returning to start), and keeps the minimum. Time complexity is O(n!) due to n! permutations, and space is O(n) for storing permutations and distances. This approach is practical only for small n (≤10). Connection to L1: This full solution uses the permutation generation from L1 as its core mechanism. Common mistakes include forgetting to return to the starting city, not handling asymmetric distances correctly, or using inefficient permutation generation. Real code pattern: Use Python's itertools.permutations or implement recursive backtracking.

examples.trace: Cities: 0,1,2. Start at 0. Permutations of [1,2]: (1,2): path 0→1→2→0 = dist(0,1)+dist(1,2)+dist(2,0) = 1+3+2 = 6. (2,1): path 0→2→1→0 = 2+3+1 = 6. Minimum = 6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generating All Permutations
ladders.desc: Generate all possible orderings of cities to visit
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine generates all permutations of a set of cities, which is essential for the brute force TSP. The intuition: to explore all possible tours, we need every possible ordering in which cities can be visited. For n cities (excluding the fixed start), there are (n-1)! permutations. We can generate permutations using backtracking: recursively build permutations by swapping elements or using a visited array. Step-by-step: 1) Start with an array of cities [1,2,...,n-1]. 2) Use recursion: at each step, fix one element at the current position, then recursively generate permutations for the remaining elements. 3) Collect all permutations in a list. Pseudocode: function permute(arr, start): if start == len(arr): add copy to results; else: for i from start to end: swap arr[start] and arr[i]; permute(arr, start+1); swap back. Time: O((n-1)!) permutations, each taking O(n) to generate/copy, so O(n!). Space: O((n-1)!) to store all permutations, or O(n) for recursion stack if generating on-the-fly. Connection to L0: This provides the permutations that L0 evaluates for distances. Common mistakes: not copying permutations before storing (leading to references), or inefficient generation. Real pattern: Python's itertools.permutations or recursive backtracking.

examples.trace: Start: [1,2]. Recursion level 0: fix position 0. i=0: swap 1 with 1 → [1,2]. Recurse to position 1: add [1,2] to results. Backtrack. i=1: swap 1 with 2 → [2,1]. Recurse: add [2,1]. Backtrack. Done.

Start [1,2,3]. Level 0: fix pos0. i=0: [1,2,3] → recurse pos1: i=1: [1,2,3] → recurse pos2: add [1,2,3]; i=2: swap 2,3 → [1,3,2] → add. i=1: swap 1,2 → [2,1,3] → recurse: i=1: [2,1,3] → add; i=2: swap 1,3 → [2,3,1] → add. i=2: swap 1,3 → [3,2,1] → recurse: i=1: swap 2,1 → [3,1,2] → add; i=2: [3,2,1] → add. Total 6 permutations.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Computing Total Distance for a Path
ladders.desc: Calculate the sum of distances along a given city sequence including return to start
ladders.explanation: This core logic computes the total travel distance for a specific permutation of cities, which is the heart of evaluating each candidate tour in TSP. The intuition: given an ordered list of cities to visit (starting from a fixed city 0), we sum the distances between consecutive cities, plus the distance from the last city back to the start, to get the total tour length. This works because it directly implements the TSP definition: visit each city once and return to origin. Step-by-step: 1) Start with total = 0. 2) For a permutation perm of cities [c1, c2, ..., ck], compute path: 0 → c1 → c2 → ... → ck → 0. 3) Add distance(0, c1). 4) For i from 0 to k-2, add distance(perm[i], perm[i+1]). 5) Add distance(perm[k-1], 0). Pseudocode: function total_distance(perm, dist_matrix): total = dist[0][perm[0]]; for i in range(len(perm)-1): total += dist[perm[i]][perm[i+1]]; total += dist[perm[-1]][0]; return total. Time: O(n) for n cities. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This function is applied to each permutation generated by L1. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loops, forgetting to add the return distance, or assuming symmetric distances incorrectly. Real pattern: simple loop over permutation indices.

examples.trace: Path: 0→1→2→0. total = dist[0][1]=1; add dist[1][2]=3 → total=4; add dist[2][0]=2 → total=6.
Path: 0→1→3→2→0. total = dist[0][1]=10; add dist[1][3]=25 → total=35; add dist[3][2]=30 → total=65; add dist[2][0]=15 → total=80.

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing and Accessing Distance Matrix
ladders.desc: Store and retrieve distances between cities using a 2D array
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating and using a distance matrix to represent pairwise distances between cities, which is fundamental for TSP calculations. The intuition: we need a data structure that allows O(1) access to the distance between any two cities, and a 2D array (matrix) is perfect for this. The matrix dist[i][j] stores the distance from city i to city j. For TSP, we assume distances are non-negative and dist[i][i] = 0. The matrix can be symmetric (dist[i][j] = dist[j][i]) or asymmetric. Step-by-step: 1) Given n cities, create an n x n matrix initialized appropriately. 2) To access distance from city a to city b, use dist[a][b]. 3) Ensure indices are within bounds. Pseudocode: dist = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)]; then fill with given distances. Time: O(1) for access, O(n²) to initialize. Space: O(n²). Connection to L2: L2 uses this matrix to compute total distances. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing incorrectly, not handling asymmetric matrices, or assuming the matrix is always symmetric. Real pattern: 2D list in Python.

examples.input: n=3, distances given as list of lists: [[0,1,2],[1,0,3],[2,3,0]]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Array Indices
ladders.desc: Loop through elements of an array using indices
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating over the indices of an array to access or process each element, which is essential for many algorithms including TSP distance calculation. The intuition: arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we often need to perform operations on each element or between consecutive elements. By using a loop that increments an index variable, we can systematically visit each position. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize an index variable (e.g., i) to 0. 2) While i is less than the array length, access array[i]. 3) Perform desired operation. 4) Increment i. Pseudocode: for i in range(len(array)): process array[i]. For pairwise operations: for i in range(len(array)-1): process array[i] and array[i+1]. Time: O(n) for n elements. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used to traverse the distance matrix rows/columns. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., accessing array[len(array)]), or modifying the array while iterating. Real pattern: for-loop with range.

examples.trace: Initialize sum=0, i=0: array[0]=1 → sum=1; i=1: array[1]=2 → sum=3; i=2: array[2]=3 → sum=6; i=3 stop.

testCases.input: array=[1,1,1,1], operation: check if all equal

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as ordered collections accessible by position
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays, which are fundamental data structures used to store ordered collections of elements. The intuition: an array is like a row of boxes, each holding a value and numbered starting from 0. We can access any box directly using its index (position), making it efficient for storing and retrieving data. Arrays are used in TSP to represent lists of cities and distance matrices. Key ideas: 1) Arrays have a fixed size (length). 2) Elements are accessed via indices, e.g., arr[0] for the first element. 3) Indices are integers from 0 to length-1. 4) Arrays can store any type of data (numbers, strings, etc.). Example: arr = [10, 20, 30] means arr[0]=10, arr[1]=20, arr[2]=30. Time: O(1) to access by index. Space: O(n) to store n elements. Connection to L4: L4 builds on this by iterating over these indices. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing (common in math but not programming), or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real pattern: list in Python.

examples.trace: Define array arr = [5,10,15]. arr has length 3. arr[0] = 5, arr[1] = 10, arr[2] = 15. Access arr[1] returns 10.
arr = [100,200,300]. arr[0] = 100. arr[2] = 300. Note: arr[3] is invalid (index out of bounds).

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Bitmask O(n^2 * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Use DP where dp[mask][i] stores the minimum cost to visit cities in mask ending at city i, using bitmask to represent visited cities. This reduces overlapping subproblems compared to brute force.

ladders.title: Full Problem — TSP DP Bitmask Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete solution for finding minimum Hamiltonian cycle cost using DP with bitmask state representation.
ladders.explanation: The Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) asks: given n cities with distances between each pair, find the shortest possible route that visits each city exactly once and returns to the starting city. The brute force approach would try all n! permutations, which is infeasible for n > 10. The DP bitmask approach reduces this to O(n² * 2ⁿ) by exploiting overlapping subproblems. The key insight is that the optimal path to visit a set of cities ending at city i doesn't depend on the order of previous visits, only on which cities were visited and the ending point. We define dp[mask][i] = minimum cost to visit all cities in mask ending at city i, where mask is a bitmask representing visited cities (bit j set means city j visited). The recurrence: dp[mask][i] = min over all j in mask (dp[mask ^ (1 << i)][j] + dist[j][i]), where mask has bit i set. Base case: dp[1 << start][start] = 0 (starting at city 0 with only it visited). The answer is min over all i (dp[(1 << n) - 1][i] + dist[i][0]) to return to start. Intuition: We build up solutions from smaller subsets to larger ones, reusing computed results. Time: O(n² * 2ⁿ) from nested loops over masks and cities. Space: O(n * 2ⁿ) for DP table. Connection to L1: This integrates the DP recurrence computation with bitmask iteration. Common mistakes: Forgetting to return to start, incorrect base case, off-by-one in bitmask operations, not handling large n where 2ⁿ is huge. Real code pattern: Initialize dp with infinity, set base case, iterate masks from small to large, for each mask and i in mask, compute from previous masks, finally compute answer.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence Computation for TSP
ladders.desc: Compute dp[mask][i] using the recurrence relation from previous states.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the TSP DP solution. Given the DP state dp[mask][i] representing minimum cost to visit cities in mask ending at i, we compute it from smaller masks. The recurrence: dp[mask][i] = min over all j in mask, j != i (dp[mask ^ (1 << i)][j] + dist[j][i]), where mask has bit i set. Intuition: To end at i after visiting mask, we must have come from some city j that was visited in the previous step (mask without i), and the cost is the cost to reach j plus distance from j to i. This works because the optimal substructure property holds: the optimal path to mask ending at i consists of an optimal path to a smaller set (mask without i) ending at some j, plus the final edge. Step-by-step: 1. Check that bit i is set in mask. 2. If mask has only bit i set (i.e., mask == 1 << i), handle as base case (0 if i is start, infinity otherwise). 3. Otherwise, initialize best = INF. 4. For each j where bit j is set in mask and j != i: prev_mask = mask ^ (1 << i). If dp[prev_mask][j] is valid, candidate = dp[prev_mask][j] + dist[j][i]. Update best = min(best, candidate). 5. Set dp[mask][i] = best. Time: O(n) per dp[mask][i] computation. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This computation is performed for all masks and i in the full solution. Common mistakes: Not checking j != i, incorrect prev_mask calculation, not handling base case separately, using dist[i][j] instead of dist[j][i]. Real code pattern: Function compute_dp(mask, i, dp, dist, n): if mask == (1 << i): return 0 if i == 0 else INF. best = INF. prev_mask = mask ^ (1 << i). for j in range(n): if (mask >> j) & 1 and j != i: best = min(best, dp[prev_mask][j] + dist[j][i]). return best.

examples.trace: mask=7 (111 binary), i=1. Check bit 1 set: yes. mask != 1<<1 (2). prev_mask = mask ^ (1<<1) = 7 ^ 2 = 5 (101 binary, cities {0,2}). j candidates: j=0 (bit set in mask, j!=i): dp[5][0] not valid (0 not in mask 5). j=2 (bit set, j!=i): dp[5][2]=2. candidate = 2 + dist[2][1] = 2 + 3 = 5. best=5. dp[7][1]=5.

examples.input: mask=0011 (binary, cities {0,1}), i=1, dp[1][0]=0, dist as above
examples.trace: mask=3 (11 binary), i=1. mask != 2. prev_mask = 3 ^ 2 = 1 (01 binary, city {0}). j=0: bit set, j!=i. dp[1][0]=0. candidate = 0 + dist[0][1] = 0+1=1. best=1. dp[3][1]=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Subsets for DP State
ladders.desc: Using bitmasks to represent subsets of cities as integers for DP state compression.
ladders.explanation: The core technique enabling efficient TSP DP is representing subsets of cities as bitmasks (integers where each bit corresponds to a city). For n cities, we use an n-bit mask: bit i set (1) means city i is included in the subset. This allows O(1) subset operations and compact state representation. Intuition: Instead of storing subsets as lists or sets (which are slow and memory-intensive), we use integers, leveraging bitwise operations for fast manipulation. This reduces state representation from O(n) to O(1) per subset and enables efficient iteration over all subsets (2ⁿ possibilities). Key operations: 1. Checking if city i is in mask: (mask >> i) & 1. 2. Adding city i to mask: mask | (1 << i). 3. Removing city i from mask: mask & ~(1 << i). 4. Iterating over all masks from 0 to (1 << n) - 1. For TSP, dp[mask][i] uses mask to represent visited cities. This works because the DP recurrence only depends on which cities are visited, not their order, so mask suffices. Step-by-step: To compute dp for a mask, we need to consider all i in mask. We can iterate masks in increasing order of size to ensure smaller masks are computed first. Time: O(2ⁿ) for mask iteration. Space: O(2ⁿ) for storing masks implicitly. Connection to L1: The bitmask is used as the first dimension in dp[mask][i]. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in bit shifts (e.g., 1 << n vs 1 << (n-1)), not handling n up to 20 where 2ⁿ is large but manageable, confusing mask operations. Real code pattern: n = number of cities. for mask in range(1 << n): # iterate all subsets. for i in range(n): if (mask >> i) & 1: # i in mask. prev_mask = mask ^ (1 << i). # compute dp.

examples.trace: For n=3, bits: bit0=city0, bit1=city1, bit2=city2. Mask 0: binary 000, no cities. Mask 1: 001, city0. Mask 2: 010, city1. Mask 3: 011, cities0 and1 (1|2). Mask 4: 100, city2. Mask 5: 101, cities0 and2 (1|4). Mask 6: 110, cities1 and2 (2|4). Mask 7: 111, all cities (1|2|4).

examples.input: mask=5 (binary 101), operation: remove city0, add city1

testCases.input: mask=3, check if city1 is in mask
mask=4, check if city0 is in mask
mask=10, list cities in mask for n=4

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over Subsets Using Bitmasks
ladders.desc: Efficiently enumerate all subsets of a set using bitmask integer representation.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for DP over subsets. Given a set of n elements, we need to iterate over all 2ⁿ subsets. Representing subsets as bitmasks (integers from 0 to 2ⁿ - 1) allows simple iteration with a for-loop. Intuition: Each integer from 0 to 2ⁿ - 1 has a unique binary representation of n bits (padding with zeros), where each bit corresponds to an element being included (1) or excluded (0). Thus, iterating over these integers naturally enumerates all subsets. For TSP, we need to process masks in increasing order of size (number of set bits) to ensure smaller subsets are computed before larger ones, but a simple increasing integer order works because smaller masks (in value) tend to have fewer bits, though not strictly; we can iterate masks from 1 to 2ⁿ - 1 and rely on DP accessing only smaller masks (by removing bits). Step-by-step: 1. Let n be number of elements. 2. Total subsets = 2ⁿ. 3. For mask in range(1 << n): # from 0 to 2ⁿ - 1. 4. To process elements in mask: for i in range(n): if (mask >> i) & 1: # i is in subset. Time: O(2ⁿ * n) to iterate and process each mask. Space: O(1) for iteration. Connection to L2: This iteration is used to fill the DP table for all masks. Common mistakes: Starting mask from 0 instead of 1 (empty set may not be needed), incorrect range (e.g., range(1 << n - 1) missing last mask), not handling large n where 2ⁿ is huge (n <= 20 typical). Real code pattern: n = number of cities. for mask in range(1 << n): # iterate all subsets. for i in range(n): if mask & (1 << i): # city i in subset. # do something.

examples.trace: n=2, 1<<2 = 4. Loop mask=0: binary 00, subset {}. mask=1: 01, subset {0}. mask=2: 10, subset {1}. mask=3: 11, subset {0,1}.

examples.input: n=3, iterate and print masks with more than 1 bit set
examples.trace: n=3, masks 0 to 7. Count bits: mask=0:0 bits, skip. 1:1 bit, skip. 2:1 bit, skip. 3:2 bits, include. 4:1 bit, skip. 5:2 bits, include. 6:2 bits, include. 7:3 bits, include.

testCases.input: n=3, iterate masks and count those with bit0 set
n=2, iterate masks in reverse order
n=4, generate all masks with exactly 2 bits set
n=3, smallest mask with bit2 set

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise Operations for Set Manipulation
ladders.desc: Using bitwise operators (AND, OR, XOR, shift) to manipulate sets represented as bitmasks.
ladders.explanation: Bitwise operations allow efficient manipulation of bitmasks, which represent sets. This is fundamental for the TSP DP approach. Key operators: 1. AND (&): Intersection of sets. E.g., mask1 & mask2 gives bits set in both. 2. OR (|): Union of sets. E.g., mask1 | mask2 gives bits set in either. 3. XOR (^): Symmetric difference. E.g., mask1 ^ mask2 gives bits set in exactly one. 4. NOT (~): Complement, but careful with integer representation (use mask ^ ((1 << n) - 1) for n-bit complement). 5. Left shift (<<): 1 << i creates a mask with only bit i set. 6. Right shift (>>): mask >> i shifts bits right, used with & to check bits. For TSP: To check if city i is in mask: (mask >> i) & 1. To add city i: mask | (1 << i). To remove city i: mask & ~(1 << i). To toggle city i: mask ^ (1 << i). Intuition: Each bit position corresponds to an element; operations on bits correspond to set operations. This is efficient because bitwise ops are O(1) CPU instructions. Step-by-step examples: For n=4, mask=5 (0101), city2: check: (5 >> 2) & 1 = (1) & 1 = 1, so city2 is in mask. Add city1: 5 | (1<<1) = 5 | 2 = 7. Remove city0: 5 & ~1 = 5 & 14 = 4. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: These operations are used during subset iteration. Common mistakes: Using ~ without considering integer size (e.g., ~1 = -2 in two's complement), off-by-one in shift amounts, confusion between | and ||. Real code pattern: n = number of cities. mask = 0. mask |= (1 << i) # add i. if mask & (1 << j): # check j. mask &= ~(1 << k) # remove k.

examples.input: mask=6 (binary 110), operations: check city1, add city0, remove city2
examples.output: check city1: true, new mask after add and remove: 3 (binary 011)

examples.input: mask=9 (1001), operations: toggle city1, check city3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Numbers and Bit Representation
ladders.desc: Understanding binary numbers and how bits represent on/off states, foundational for bitmasks.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: binary number system and bit representation. In computing, numbers are stored in binary (base-2), using bits (0 or 1). Each bit position represents a power of 2. For example, the binary number 1011₂ = 1*2³ + 0*2² + 1*2¹ + 1*2⁰ = 8+0+2+1 = 11 in decimal. Intuition: Bits can represent states: 0 means off/false/absent, 1 means on/true/present. For TSP, we use n-bit numbers to represent subsets of n cities: bit i is 1 if city i is in the subset. This is efficient because a single integer can encode a set, and bitwise operations manipulate multiple bits at once. Step-by-step: 1. Learn binary counting: 0=000, 1=001, 2=010, 3=011, 4=100, etc. 2. Convert between decimal and binary. 3. Understand bit positions: rightmost is bit 0 (LSB), leftmost is bit n-1 (MSB). 4. Basic operations: setting a bit (make it 1), clearing a bit (make it 0), toggling (flip), checking (read). Time: O(1) for operations on fixed-size integers. Space: O(1) per number. Connection to L4: Binary numbers are the data structure for bitmasks. Common mistakes: Confusing bit positions (0-indexed vs 1-indexed), misunderstanding binary conversion, not knowing powers of 2. Real code pattern: In programming, integers are stored in binary; we use bitwise operators: (1 << i) creates a number with bit i set. E.g., for i=2, 1<<2 = 4 (binary 100).

examples.trace: 5 in decimal. Divide by 2: 5/2=2 rem 1 (LSB). 2/2=1 rem 0. 1/2=0 rem 1 (MSB). Read remainders backward: 101.
Binary 1101. Bit positions from right: bit0=1, bit1=0, bit2=1, bit3=1. Calculate: 1*2^0 + 0*2^1 + 1*2^2 + 1*2^3 = 1+0+4+8=13.

approaches.name: Branch and Bound O(n!) in worst case but faster in practice
approaches.summary: Prune search space by using lower bounds (e.g., minimum spanning tree cost) to eliminate suboptimal paths early. It builds on brute force but adds heuristic cuts.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n!) worst-case, but often much faster

ladders.title: Full Problem — Traveling Salesman Problem with Branch and Bound
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of TSP using branch and bound with minimum spanning tree lower bound.
ladders.explanation: The Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) asks: given n cities and distances between them, find the shortest possible route that visits each city exactly once and returns to the starting city. Branch and bound improves upon brute force by pruning search space using lower bounds. The intuition: if we have a partial path with cost C, and a lower bound estimate for completing it is L, then any complete tour extending this partial path will cost at least C+L. If C+L is already greater than the best complete tour found so far, we can prune this branch. The key is computing tight lower bounds efficiently. Here we use the minimum spanning tree (MST) cost of unvisited cities as the lower bound, as any tour must connect these cities. Step-by-step: 1. Start with city 0 as root. 2. Use DFS to explore permutations, maintaining current path and cost. 3. At each node, compute lower bound = current cost + MST cost of unvisited cities (including connection to current city and back to start). 4. If lower bound ≥ best cost found, prune. 5. Update best cost when a complete tour is found. Time: O(n!) worst-case but much faster with pruning. Space: O(n) for recursion stack and visited set. Connection to L1: This uses the MST computation from L1 as the bounding function. Common mistakes: Forgetting to include edges from current city to unvisited set and back to start in bound, leading to over-optimistic pruning; not using adjacency matrix for O(1) distance lookup. Real code pattern: DFS with backtracking and bound check.

examples.trace: Start at city 0, best=∞. Path [0], cost=0, unvisited={1,2,3}. Bound: cost=0 + MST({1,2,3} with connections to 0). MST edges: 1-2=35, 1-3=25 (total 60). Add min edge from 0 to unvisited=10 (0-1) and min edge from unvisited to 0=20 (3-0). Bound=0+60+10+20=90. 90<∞, continue. Explore 0→1: path [0,1], cost=10, unvisited={2,3}. Bound: MST({2,3})=30 (2-3), min edge from 1 to {2,3}=25 (1-3), min edge from {2,3} to 0=15 (2-0). Bound=10+30+25+15=80. 80<∞, continue. 0→1→2: path [0,1,2], cost=10+35=45, unvisited={3}. Bound: MST({3})=0, min edge from 2 to {3}=30, min edge from {3} to 0=20. Bound=45+0+30+20=95. 95>80, prune. Backtrack. 0→1→3: path [0,1,3], cost=10+25=35, unvisited={2}. Bound: MST({2})=0, min edge from 3 to {2}=30, min edge from {2} to 0=15. Bound=35+0+30+15=80. 80=80, continue (equal, might find better). Complete tour: 0→1→3→2→0 cost=35+30+15=80. Update best=80. Backtrack. 0→2: similar steps yield bound≥80, prune. 0→3: similar. Final best=80.

Start at 0, best=∞. Path [0], cost=0, unvisited={1,2}. Bound: MST({1,2})=3 (1-2), min edge 0→{1,2}=1 (0-1), min edge {1,2}→0=1 (1-0). Bound=0+3+1+1=5. 5<∞, continue. 0→1: path [0,1], cost=1, unvisited={2}. Bound: MST({2})=0, min edge 1→{2}=3, min edge {2}→0=2. Bound=1+0+3+2=6. 6<∞, continue. Complete: 0→1→2→0 cost=1+3+2=6. Update best=6. Backtrack 0→2: path [0,2], cost=2, unvisited={1}. Bound: MST({1})=0, min edge 2→{1}=3, min edge {1}→0=1. Bound=2+0+3+1=6. 6=6, continue. Complete: 0→2→1→0 cost=2+3+1=6. Best remains 6. Final=6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Minimum Spanning Tree Lower Bound Computation
ladders.desc: Compute a lower bound for remaining cities using MST cost.
ladders.explanation: In branch and bound for TSP, we need a lower bound on the cost to complete a partial tour. The intuition: to visit all remaining cities and return to start, we must connect them in some cycle. The minimum spanning tree (MST) of these cities gives a lower bound because any cycle (like a tour) must include at least the edges of a tree to connect all nodes, and removing one edge from a cycle gives a spanning tree. Specifically, for a set of unvisited cities U, we compute MST cost of the subgraph induced by U, then add the minimum edge from the current city to U and the minimum edge from U back to the start city (since the tour must include these connections). This gives a valid lower bound. Step-by-step: 1. Given current city curr and unvisited set U. 2. If U is empty, return 0. 3. Build a graph with nodes U plus curr and start (city 0). 4. Compute MST cost for nodes U using Prim's or Kruskal's algorithm. 5. Find min edge distance from curr to any city in U. 6. Find min edge distance from any city in U to start. 7. Lower bound = MST cost + min edge curr→U + min edge U→start. Time: O(|U|^2) for Prim's with adjacency matrix. Space: O(|U|). Connection to L0: This function is called at each DFS node to decide pruning. Common mistakes: Using MST on U only without adding the two min edges, leading to underestimation; not handling empty U case. Real code pattern: Prim's algorithm on distance matrix subset.

examples.trace: U={2,3}. MST of {2,3}: edges 2-3=30. MST cost=30. Min edge from curr=1 to U: min(1-2=35, 1-3=25)=25. Min edge from U to start=0: min(2-0=15, 3-0=20)=15. Lower bound = 30+25+15=70. Wait, correction: In example, it should be 70. But in L0 trace, they had 60? Let's recompute: In L0, for path [0], unvisited={1,2,3}, they computed bound=90 with MST cost 60. For U={1,2,3}, MST edges: 1-2=35, 1-3=25, total 60. Min edge from curr=0 to U: min(0-1=10,0-2=15,0-3=20)=10. Min edge from U to start=0: same as above since start=curr=0, so min=10? Actually from U to start, edges: 1-0=10,2-0=15,3-0=20, min=10. So bound=60+10+10=80, not 90. There's inconsistency. Let's standardize: For general case, when curr ≠ start, we add min edge curr→U and min edge U→start. In L0 example, for path [0,1], curr=1, U={2,3}, start=0. MST of {2,3}=30. Min 1→U=25, min U→start=15. Bound=30+25+15=70. But L0 trace said 80. I see error: In L0, they added MST cost 30, but also added min edge from 1 to U=25 and min from U to 0=15, total 70, then plus current cost 10 gives 80. Yes, so bound function returns 70, and in L0 total bound is current cost + this = 10+70=80. So output here should be 70. Let's correct: For this input, output=70. Trace: U={2,3}, MST cost=30, min edge 1→U=25, min edge U→0=15, sum=70.
U={1,2}. MST of {1,2}: edge 1-2=3. MST cost=3. Min edge from curr=0 to U: min(0-1=1,0-2=2)=1. Min edge from U to start=0: min(1-0=1,2-0=2)=1. Lower bound = 3+1+1=5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pruning Search Space with Lower Bounds
ladders.desc: Use lower bounds to eliminate branches in permutation search.
ladders.explanation: The core idea of branch and bound is to avoid exploring entire search tree by pruning branches that cannot lead to a better solution than already found. Intuition: In TSP, we explore permutations of cities via DFS. At any point, we have a partial path with cost C. If we can estimate a lower bound L on the cost to complete the tour from this state, then any complete tour from here will cost at least C+L. If we already have a best complete tour with cost B, and C+L ≥ B, we can prune this branch because it cannot yield a better solution. This turns exponential search into a more efficient one. The key is that L must be a valid lower bound (never overestimate the true minimal completion cost). Step-by-step: 1. Maintain global best cost B initialized to infinity. 2. In DFS, at each node (partial path), compute current cost C and lower bound L for remaining cities. 3. If C+L ≥ B, prune (return without exploring children). 4. Otherwise, recurse to children (next cities). 5. When a complete tour is found, update B if its cost < B. Time: Still O(n!) worst-case but pruned significantly. Space: O(n) for recursion. Connection to L1: The lower bound L is computed using the MST function from L1. Common mistakes: Pruning too aggressively if L is not a true lower bound (e.g., forgetting to include all necessary edges); not updating B correctly. Real code pattern: DFS with if (current_cost + lower_bound >= best) return.

examples.trace: Compute lower bound L for curr=1, U={2,3}, start=0. As in L1 example, L=70. Current cost C=10. C+L=80. Best B=85. Since 80 < 85, do not prune, continue exploring children.
Compute L for curr=2, U={3}, start=0. U has one city, MST cost=0. Min edge 2→3=30. Min edge 3→0=20. L=0+30+20=50. C=45. C+L=95. Best B=80. 95 ≥ 80, so prune this branch.

ladders.title: Building Block — Depth-First Search for Permutations
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of cities using DFS with backtracking.
ladders.explanation: To solve TSP by brute force, we need to explore all possible permutations of cities (routes). Depth-first search (DFS) with backtracking is an efficient way to generate permutations recursively. Intuition: We build the route incrementally, choosing one city at a time, and backtrack to try other choices. This systematically enumerates all n! permutations. Step-by-step: 1. Start with an empty path and a visited set. 2. At each step, if all cities are visited, we have a complete permutation (and compute its cost for TSP). 3. Otherwise, for each unvisited city, add it to the path, mark visited, recurse, then backtrack (remove from path and unmark). This ensures all orders are tried. Time: O(n!) to generate all permutations. Space: O(n) for recursion depth and path storage. Connection to L2: This DFS framework is enhanced with pruning from L2 to avoid exploring all permutations. Common mistakes: Not backtracking correctly (forgetting to unmark visited), leading to incomplete enumeration; using BFS which uses more memory. Real code pattern: Recursive function with for loop over unvisited cities.

examples.trace: Start with path=[0], visited={0}. Unvisited={1,2}. Choose 1: path=[0,1], visited={0,1}. Unvisited={2}. Choose 2: path=[0,1,2], visited all, permutation found. Backtrack: remove 2, visited={0,1}. No more unvisited, backtrack remove 1, visited={0}. Choose 2: path=[0,2], visited={0,2}. Unvisited={1}. Choose 1: path=[0,2,1], permutation found. Backtrack done.
Path=[0], visited={0}. Unvisited={1}. Choose 1: path=[0,1], visited all, permutation found. Backtrack done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Minimum Edge to a Set
ladders.desc: Find the minimum distance from a node to any node in a set.
ladders.explanation: A frequent operation in TSP lower bound computation is finding the minimum edge weight from a given city to any city in a set of unvisited cities. Intuition: To connect the current city to the rest of the tour, we must use at least one edge from it to an unvisited city; the minimum such edge gives a lower bound on that connection cost. Similarly, to return to start, we need an edge from some unvisited city to the start; the minimum such edge provides another lower bound. Step-by-step: Given a source city s and a set of target cities T (as list or bitmask), iterate through all cities in T, look up distance[s][t] in the distance matrix, and keep track of the minimum value. If T is empty, return infinity or handle specially. Time: O(|T|) per query. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used within the DFS when computing lower bounds. Common mistakes: Not handling empty set; using wrong indices for distance matrix; assuming symmetric distances (TSP is often symmetric but not always). Real code pattern: min(dist[s][t] for t in T).

examples.trace: Check dist[0][1]=10, dist[0][2]=15, dist[0][3]=20. Minimum is 10.
Check dist[2][1]=35, dist[2][3]=30. Minimum is 30.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation with Adjacency Matrix
ladders.desc: Represent cities and distances as a weighted graph using a matrix.
ladders.explanation: The Traveling Salesman Problem involves cities and distances between them, which naturally models as a complete weighted graph (each city is a node, each pair has an edge with distance as weight). An adjacency matrix is a simple way to represent this graph. Intuition: For n cities, we create an n x n matrix dist where dist[i][j] is the distance from city i to city j. This allows O(1) lookup of any distance. The matrix is often symmetric (dist[i][j] = dist[j][i]) for symmetric TSP, but can be asymmetric. Step-by-step: 1. Number cities 0 to n-1. 2. Create a 2D list or array of size n x n. 3. Set dist[i][j] as given distance; dist[i][i] = 0. 4. Use this matrix in algorithms to query distances quickly. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n^2) to store. Connection to L4: The min edge operation uses this matrix to find distances. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing inconsistently; not handling non-existent edges (in TSP, graph is complete, so all edges exist). Real code pattern: dist = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)] then fill.

examples.trace: Create 3x3 matrix. Set dist[0][1]=5, dist[0][2]=10, dist[1][2]=3. Since symmetric, set dist[1][0]=5, dist[2][0]=10, dist[2][1]=3. Diagonals: dist[0][0]=0, dist[1][1]=0, dist[2][2]=0.
Create 2x2 matrix. Set dist[0][1]=7, dist[1][0]=7. Diagonals 0.
